t: SOGS (South Oaks Gambling Screen) or PGSI (Problem Gambling Severity Index).
A persistent pattern across all domains.
CBT — first line; trigger management, behavioural activation, cognitive restructuring ("near miss" and "gambler's fallacy"). Cowlishaw S. Cochrane 2012 meta-analysis.
Naltrexone, nalmefene — the evidence base is evolving (reviews by Grant J.E.).
For gaming — physical activity, alternative pursuits, family intervention.
— Correction of cognitive distortions, exposure (reducing responses to triggers), problem-solving.
Comorbid MDD predicts a poor prognosis.
DSM-5, ICD-11 — it is a medical/behavioural disorder; neurobiological and genetic predisposition.
Myth 2: "The patient will get out of debt with a "big win""
"chasing losses" is a diagnostic feature of gambling disorder; the pattern of losses is statistically inevitable.
Myth 3: "Gaming is not an addiction, just a hobby"
WHO — Gaming Disorder is official; functional impairment and neurobiological similarity.
reliance on bailouts is a diagnostic feature; psychosocial intervention is required.
Myth 5: "The patient will "grow out of it""
active intervention is required; risk of chronicity.
Cowlishaw S. et al. Psychological therapies for pathological and problem gambling. Cochrane Database Syst Rev 2012;(11):CD008937.
Grant J.E. et al. Pharmacotherapy for gambling disorder. Curr Top Behav Neurosci 2016;26:135–149.
